From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Traditional naming system in Vietnam

For toponyms, see [Place names of Vietnam](/wiki/Place_names_of_Vietnam "Place names of Vietnam").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Vietnamese_name "Special:EditPage/Vietnamese name") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Vietnamese name"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Vietnamese+name%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Vietnamese+name%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Vietnamese+name%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Vietnamese+name%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Vietnamese+name%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Vietnamese+name%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Vietnamese names** ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *họ tên*) generally consist of two components including a "surname" and a "given name", follow the [Eastern name order](/wiki/Personal_name#Eastern_name_order "Personal name"):

* A [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname"): most consist of a [monosyllable](/wiki/Monosyllable "Monosyllable") and normally [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal"), although [matrilineality](/wiki/Matrilineality "Matrilineality") is possible.
* A [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") including:
  + An optional [middle name](#Middle_name_(padding_name)) that is typically a monosyllable, although some individuals have a multisyllabic one or none at all.
  + A primary name usually consists of one or two [syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") as a [double name](/wiki/Double_name "Double name").

However, not every name is conformant. For example:

* *[Nguyễn Trãi](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Tr%C3%A3i "Nguyễn Trãi")* has his surname *[Nguyễn](/wiki/Nguyen "Nguyen")* and his primary name is *Trãi*. He does not have any middle name.
* *[Phạm Bình Minh](/wiki/Ph%E1%BA%A1m_B%C3%ACnh_Minh "Phạm Bình Minh")* has his surname *[Phạm](/wiki/Ph%E1%BA%A1m "Phạm")* and his primary name is *Bình Minh* (lit. '[dawn](/wiki/Dawn "Dawn")'). He does not have any middle name, although *Bình* is often mistaken for one.
* *[Nguyễn Văn Quyết](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_V%C4%83n_Quy%E1%BA%BFt "Nguyễn Văn Quyết")* has his surname *Nguyễn*, his middle name is *Văn* and his primary name is *Quyết* (lit. 'decide').
* *[Nguyễn Ngọc Trường Sơn](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ng%E1%BB%8Dc_Tr%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Dng_S%C6%A1n "Nguyễn Ngọc Trường Sơn")* has his surname *Nguyễn*, his middle name is *Ngọc* (lit. '[gemstone](/wiki/Gemstone "Gemstone")') and his primary name is *Trường Sơn* (lit. 'long mountain')
* *[Lâm Thị Mỹ Dạ](/wiki/L%C3%A2m_Th%E1%BB%8B_M%E1%BB%B9_D%E1%BA%A1 "Lâm Thị Mỹ Dạ")* has her surname *[Lâm](/wiki/L%C3%A2m "Lâm")*, her middle name is *Thị* and her primary name is *Mỹ Dạ* (lit. 'beautiful night'). Her husband, *Hoàng Phủ Ngọc Tường* (a Vietnamese poet),[[1]](#cite_note-1) has his surname is [*Hoàng Phủ*](/wiki/Huangfu "Huangfu") (natural [compound surname](/wiki/Compound_family_name "Compound family name")), his middle name is *Ngọc* and his primary name is *Tường* (lit. 'deep understanding'). As almost every Vietnamese surname is monosyllablic, his surname is usually confused with *[Hoàng](/wiki/Ho%C3%A0ng "Hoàng")*, leading to their two daughters being named as *Hoàng Dạ Thi* and *Hoàng Dạ Thư* instead of *Hoàng Phủ Dạ Thi* and *Hoàng Phủ Dạ Thư*.
* *[Trần Lê Quốc Toàn](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_L%C3%AA_Qu%E1%BB%91c_To%C3%A0n "Trần Lê Quốc Toàn")* has his surname *[Trần](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n "Trần")*, his first middle name [*Lê*](/wiki/Le_(surname) "Le (surname)") is derived from his mother's surname, his second middle name is *Quốc* (lit. 'country') and his primary name is *Toàn* (lit. 'fully').

The "family name first" written order is usual throughout the [East Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/East_Asian_cultural_sphere "East Asian cultural sphere") which Vietnam is a part of. Persons can be referred to by the whole name, the primary name, or [a hierarchic pronoun](/wiki/Vietnamese_pronouns#Kinship_terms "Vietnamese pronouns"), which usually connotes a degree of family relationship or kinship – but referring via the primary name is most common, as well as if degree of family relationship or kinship is unknown. In more informal contexts or in the Western world, the primary name can be written first then surname e.g. *[Tam Dinh](/wiki/Tam_Dinh "Tam Dinh")* or *[Khoa Tran](/wiki/Khoa_Tran "Khoa Tran")*.

The [Vietnamese language](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") is tonal and so are Vietnamese names. Names with the same spelling but different tones represent different meanings, which can confuse people when the [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") are dropped, as is commonly done outside Vietnam (e.g. [*Đoàn*](/wiki/Duan_(surname) "Duan (surname)") ([[ɗʷà:n]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Vietnamese "Help:IPA/Vietnamese")) vs [*Doãn*](/wiki/Y%C7%90n_(surname) "Yǐn (surname)") ([[zʷǎ:ˀn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Vietnamese "Help:IPA/Vietnamese")), both become *Doan* when diacritics are omitted). Additionally, due to [homonymy](/wiki/Homonym "Homonym"), some Vietnamese names can only be distinguished through context or by reference to their corresponding [chữ Hán](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_H%C3%A1n "Chữ Hán"), such as 南 (lit. 'south') or 男 (lit. 'men, boy'), both are read as *Nam*. Anyone applying for Vietnamese nationality must also adopt a Vietnamese name.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Vietnamese names have corresponding Hán character [adopted](/wiki/Calque "Calque") early on during [Chinese rule](/wiki/Vietnam_under_Chinese_rule "Vietnam under Chinese rule"). The modern Vietnamese writing system, *[chữ Quốc ngữ](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_Qu%E1%BB%91c_ng%E1%BB%AF "Chữ Quốc ngữ")*, popularized during the [French colonial era](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina"), is fully romanized and has replaced the earlier, Chinese character–based script, *[chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm")*.

## Surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Surnames")]

The surname ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *họ*) is positioned first and is passed on by the father to his children in a traditionally [patrilineal order](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal"), but exceptions are possible. It is estimated that there are around 100 surnames in common use, but some are far more common than others. The name *[Nguyễn](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n "Nguyễn")* was estimated to be the most common (40%) in 2005.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The reason the top three names are so common is that people tended to take the surnames of emperors, to show loyalty to particular dynasties in history. Over many generations, those surnames became permanent.

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Some scholars argue that all Vietnamese surnames are of Chinese origin, introduced during the thousand-year [Chinese occupation of Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_under_Chinese_rule "Vietnam under Chinese rule"), which began in 111 BCE with the [Han Dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Prior to this, evidence of distinct Vietnamese surnames is scarce due to a lack of written records.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) An alternative view suggests that Vietnamese surnames include both indigenous names and those borrowed from [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Hypotheses propose that indigenous surnames may have evolved from place-names in the Red River delta (e.g., *Nguyễn* from "nguồn, ngòi" meaning spring or canal) or from traditional totems (e.g., *Gà* for "chicken tribe").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Historically, individuals sometimes adopted the surname of the ruling dynasty as a sign of loyalty, or were compelled to do so, particularly after dynastic changes.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) For example, during the [Trần dynasty](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_dynasty "Trần dynasty"), individuals with the surname Lý (from the overthrown Lý dynasty) were ordered to change their surname to Nguyễn.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) The [Nguyễn dynasty](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_dynasty "Nguyễn dynasty") (1802-1945) further contributed to the prevalence of the Nguyễn surname.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Additionally, surnames were sometimes changed to evade taxes, avoid penalties, or adhere to royal name taboos.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

Vietnamese surnames also have origins from other ethnic groups, including [Chinese](/wiki/Hoa_people "Hoa people") (*Khổng, Lưu, Trương*), [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_people "Khmer people") (*Thạch, Sơn*), [Cham](/wiki/Chams "Chams") (*Chế, Chiêm*), and various ethnic minorities (*Linh, Giáp, Ma*).[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

### Common surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Common surnames")]

The following are the most common surnames among Vietnamese, with their [*chữ Quốc ngữ*](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet") spelling, and their corresponding Hán-Nôm characters.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The figures are from a 2022 study *100 họ phổ biến ở Việt Nam* (100 Most Popular Surnames In Vietnam) from the Vietnamese Social Science Publisher (*Nhà xuất bản Khoa học Xã hội*). In 2005, these 14 names had accounted for around 90% of the Vietnamese population.

2. Trần (10.9%)
3. Lê (8.90%)
4. Phạm (5.90%)
5. Hoàng / Huỳnh (5.10%)

In Vietnamese culture, women keep their surnames after marriage. Even though it is not required by law, children usually bear the father’s surname. After the French colonial period, there emerged a trend in which someone is given a middle name derived from the mother’s surname as a gesture of respect and remembrance (e.g. *[Trần](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n "Trần") [Lê](/wiki/L%C3%AA "Lê") [Quốc Toàn](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_L%C3%AA_Qu%E1%BB%91c_To%C3%A0n "Trần Lê Quốc Toàn")*).

In more casual contexts, people are always on a "first-name basis", which involves their primary names, accompanied by proper [kinship terms](/wiki/Vietnamese_pronouns#Kinship_terms "Vietnamese pronouns").

## Given names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Given names")]

The given name ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *tên or tên riêng*) is consist of an optional middle name and a mandatory primary name.

### Middle name (padding name)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Middle name (padding name)")]

Middle name, which is known officially as "Padding name" ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *tên đệm, tên lót or chữ đệm*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") '*padding name* or *padding character*') in Vietnamese is optional. Although placed in the "middle" position in a full name, Vietnamese middle name has a very different role and usage compared to Western one: as the [prefix](/wiki/Prefix "Prefix") to supplement the primary name, it cannot be used independently and must be used together with the primary name when addressing a person. Therefore, middle name in Vietnamese is considered part of the given name.

* For example, *[Jacob Harry Marguire](/wiki/Jacob_Harry_Maguire "Jacob Harry Maguire")* is commonly known as "Harry Marguire" and he can be addressed simply by his middle name "Harry". Or [*Andrea Kimi Antonelli*](/wiki/Andrea_Kimi_Antonelli "Andrea Kimi Antonelli") is commonly known as "Kimi Antonelli" and he can be addressed simply by his middle name "Kimi". However in Vietnamese custom, *[Nguyễn Văn Toàn](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_V%C4%83n_To%C3%A0n_(footballer,_born_1996) "Nguyễn Văn Toàn (footballer, born 1996)")* is often addressed by primary name "Toàn" or formally "Văn Toàn", never addressed by only middle name "Văn".

Most Vietnamese people have a monosyllablic middle name, but it is also quite common to have multisyllable or none at all such as *[Tô Lâm](/wiki/T%C3%B4_L%C3%A2m "Tô Lâm")*. Semantically, a middle name can stand alone (e.g., *Văn* or *Thị*), but it is usually combined with the primary name to form a more meaningful full name, where the middle name functions as part of the primary name.

In the past, the middle name was selected by parents from a fairly narrow range of options. Almost all women had *Thị* (氏) as their middle name, and many men had *Văn* (文). More recently, a broader range of names has been used, and people named *Thị* usually omit their middle name because they do not like to call it with their name. For example, singer [Hồ Ngọc Hà](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_Ng%E1%BB%8Dc_H%C3%A0 "Hồ Ngọc Hà") has birthname "Hồ Thị Ngọc Hà". *Thị* is a most common female middle name, and most common amongst pre-1975 generation but less common amongst younger generations. *Thị* (氏) is an archaic [Sino-Vietnamese](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_vocabulary "Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary") suffix meaning "clan; family; lineage; hereditary house" and attached to a woman's original surname, but now is used to simply indicate the female sex. For example, the name "Trần Thị Mai Loan" means "Mai Loan, a female person of the Trần family". Some traditional male middle names may include *Văn* (文), *Hữu* (友), *Đức* (德), *Thành* (誠), *Công* (公), *Minh* (明), and *Quang* (光).

The middle name can have several uses:

1. To indicate a person's generation. Brothers and sisters may share the same middle name, which distinguish them from the generation before them and the generation after them (see [generation name](/wiki/Generation_name "Generation name")).
2. To separate branches of a large family: "Nguyễn *Hữu*", "Nguyễn *Sinh*", "Trần *Lâm*" (middle names can be taken from the mother's surname). However, this usage is still controversial[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Vietnamese_name#Dubious "Talk:Vietnamese name")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Some people[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] consider them to be a part of their surnames, not surname + middle name. Some families may, however, set up arbitrary rules about giving a different middle name to each generation.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
3. To indicate a person's position ([birth order](/wiki/Birth_order "Birth order")) in the family. This usage is less common than others.
4. To provide a poetic and positive meaning. E.g. "Trần Gia Hạnh Phúc" meaning "Happiness to the Trần family".
5. Some children take the father’s (or mother's) surname, their middle name is derived from the mother's (or father's) surname as a gesture of respect and remembrance. E.g. [Trần "Lê" Quốc Toàn](/wiki/Tr%E1%BA%A7n_L%C3%AA_Qu%E1%BB%91c_To%C3%A0n "Trần Lê Quốc Toàn"), [Cao "Pendant" Quang Vinh](/wiki/Cao_Pendant_Quang_Vinh "Cao Pendant Quang Vinh").

The first three are not as common in the present-day as they are seen as too rigid and strictly conforming to family naming systems. Most middle names utilise the fourth, having a name to simply imply some positive characteristics.

### Primary name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Primary name")]

In most cases, the middle name is formally part of the primary name ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *tên chính*). For example, the name "Đinh Quang Dũng" is separated into the surname "Đinh" and the primary name "Quang Dũng". In a normal name list, those two parts of the full name are put in two different columns. However, in daily conversation, the last syllable in a primary name with a title before it is used to call or address a person: "Ông Dũng", "Anh Dũng", etc., with "Ông" and "Anh" being words to address the person and depend on age, social position, etc.

The primary name is the primary form of address for Vietnamese. It is chosen by parents and usually has a literal meaning in the [Vietnamese language](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"). Names often represent beauty, such as bird or flower names, or attributes and characteristics that the parents want in their child, such as modesty (*Khiêm*, 謙).

Typically, Vietnamese will be addressed with their primary name, even in formal situations, although an honorific equivalent to "Mr.", "Mrs.", etc. will be added when necessary. That contrasts with the situation in many other cultures in which the surname is used in formal situations, but it is a practice similar to usage in [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_name "Icelandic name") usage and, to some degree, [Polish](/wiki/Polish_name "Polish name"). It is similar to the Latin-American and southern European custom of referring to women as "[Doña/Dona](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)")" and men as "[Don/Dom](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)")", along with their first name.

Addressing someone by the surname is rare in the current. In the past, women were usually called by their (maiden) surname, with *thị* (氏) as a suffix, similar to [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In recent years, doctors are more likely than any other social group to be addressed by their surname, but that form of reference is more common in the north than in the south. Some extremely famous people are sometimes referred to by their surnames regardless of whether the name is an alias, such as [Hồ Chí Minh](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh "Ho Chi Minh") (*Bác Hồ*—"*Uncle Hồ*") (although his real name is Nguyễn Sinh Cung), [Trịnh Công Sơn](/wiki/Tr%E1%BB%8Bnh_C%C3%B4ng_S%C6%A1n "Trịnh Công Sơn") (*nhạc Trịnh*—"*Trịnh music*"), and [Hồ Xuân Hương](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_Xu%C3%A2n_H%C6%B0%C6%A1ng "Hồ Xuân Hương") (*nữ sĩ họ Hồ*—"*the poetess with the surname Hồ*"). Traditionally, people in Vietnam, particularly North Vietnam, addressed parents using the first child's name.

When being addressed within the family, children are sometimes referred to by their birth number, starting with one in the north but two in the south. That practice is less common recently, especially in the north.

[Double names](/wiki/Double_name "Double name") are also common. For example:

* *[Trương Mỹ Hoa](/wiki/Tr%C6%B0%C6%A1ng_M%E1%BB%B9_Hoa "Trương Mỹ Hoa")* has the primary name *Mỹ Hoa* (lit. 'beautiful flower'). She doesn't have any middle name, although "Mỹ" is often mistaken for one.
* *[Nguyễn Thị Kim Ngân](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Th%E1%BB%8B_Kim_Ng%C3%A2n "Nguyễn Thị Kim Ngân")* has the primary name *Kim Ngân* (lit. 'gold & silver'). She has a middle name "Thị", however "Kim" is also often mistaken for an additional one.

## Non-traditional names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Non-traditional names")]

### Foreign names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Foreign names")]

If either the father or mother is a foreigner and is married to a Vietnamese citizen, and their child is born in Vietnam and registered with Vietnamese nationality from the outset, the child may take the surname of either the foreign parent or the Vietnamese parent, however, the given name (underlining) must be Vietnamese,[[6]](#cite_note-6) such as "[Smith](/wiki/Smith_(surname) "Smith (surname)") Tuấn Anh" (or *Tuan-Anh Smith* in Western order) is acceptable, but "Nguyễn David" (*David Nguyen*) is not. For those born abroad who wish to acquire Vietnamese citizenship, they may keep their foreign name (regardless of whether it is the surname or the given name in the original name) after becoming Vietnamese citizens, such as "[Hoàng Vũ Samson](/wiki/Ho%C3%A0ng_V%C5%A9_Samson "Hoàng Vũ Samson")", "[Huỳnh Kesley Alves](/wiki/Hu%E1%BB%B3nh_Kesley_Alves "Huỳnh Kesley Alves")", "[Nguyễn Filip](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Filip "Nguyễn Filip")", "[Cao Pendant Quang Vinh](/wiki/Cao_Pendant_Quang_Vinh "Cao Pendant Quang Vinh")".

### Saints' names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Saints' names")]

[Vietnamese Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Vietnam "Catholic Church in Vietnam") are given a [saint's name](/wiki/Saint%27s_name "Saint's name") at [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"): *tên thánh (holy name)* or *tên rửa tội (baptism name)*). Boys are given male saints' names, while girls are given female saints' names. This name appears first, before the surname, in formal religious contexts. Out of respect, clergy are usually referred to by saints' name. The saint's name also functions as a [posthumous name](/wiki/Posthumous_name "Posthumous name"), used instead of an individual's personal name in prayers after their death. The most common saints' names are taken from the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), such as *Phêrô* (Peter, or Pierre in French), *Phaolô* (Paul), *Gioan* (John), *Maria* (Mary), and *Anna* or they may remain as they are without Vietnamisation.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Saints' names are respelled phonetically according to the [Vietnamese alphabet](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet"). Some more well-known saints' names are derived further into names that sound more Vietnamese or easier to pronounce for Vietnamese speakers.

## Near-homonyms distinguished by vowel or tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Near-homonyms distinguished by vowel or tones")]

Some names may appear the same if simplified into a basic [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") script, as for example on websites, but are different names:

* [Trịnh Căn](/wiki/Tr%E1%BB%8Bnh_C%C4%83n "Trịnh Căn") (鄭根, 1633–1709) reformist warlord, vs. [Trịnh Cán](/wiki/Tr%E1%BB%8Bnh_C%C3%A1n "Trịnh Cán") (鄭檊, 1777–1782) infant heir of warlord Trịnh Sâm
* [Nguyễn Du](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Du "Nguyễn Du") (1765–1820) writer, vs. [Nguyễn Dữ](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_D%E1%BB%AF "Nguyễn Dữ") (c.1550) writer
* [Hoàng Tích Chu](/wiki/Ho%C3%A0ng_T%C3%ADch_Chu "Hoàng Tích Chu") (1897–1933) journalist, vs. [Hoàng Tích Chù](/wiki/Ho%C3%A0ng_T%C3%ADch_Ch%C3%B9 "Hoàng Tích Chù") (1912–2003) painter
* [Nguyễn Văn Tỵ](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_V%C4%83n_T%E1%BB%B5 "Nguyễn Văn Tỵ") (1917–1992) painter and poet, vs. [Nguyễn Văn Tý](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_V%C4%83n_T%C3%BD "Nguyễn Văn Tý") (1925–2019), composer
* [Phan Thanh Hùng](/wiki/Phan_Thanh_H%C3%B9ng "Phan Thanh Hùng") (1960) football manager, vs. [Phan Thanh Hưng](/wiki/Phan_Thanh_H%C6%B0ng "Phan Thanh Hưng") (1987), footballer
* [Nguyễn Bình](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_B%C3%ACnh "Nguyễn Bình") (1906–1951), vs. [Nguyễn Bính](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_B%C3%ADnh "Nguyễn Bính") (1918–1966)
* [Nguyễn Văn Hưng](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_V%C4%83n_H%C6%B0ng "Nguyễn Văn Hưng") (1958) representative of the Vietnam National Assembly, vs. [Nguyễn Văn Hùng](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_V%C4%83n_H%C3%B9ng_(martial_artist) "Nguyễn Văn Hùng (martial artist)") (1980), martial artist

Typically, as in the above examples, it is middle or the last primary name which varies, as almost any [Hán-Nôm](/wiki/H%C3%A1n-N%C3%B4m "Hán-Nôm") character may be used. The number of surnames is limited.

Further, some historical names may be written using different [chữ Hán](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_H%C3%A1n "Chữ Hán") ([Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters")), but are still written the same in the modern Vietnamese alphabet.

## Presentation in English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Presentation in English")]

### Indexing, sorting and name order reversal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Indexing, sorting and name order reversal")]

Due to foreigners’ limited familiarity with writing and [typing Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language_and_computers "Vietnamese language and computers") [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic"), and to avoid encoding errors that may corrupt font display, Vietnamese names are often written without diacritics in English, such as "[Ho Chi Minh](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh "Ho Chi Minh")" instead of "Hồ Chí Minh", "Vo Nguyen Giap" instead of "[Võ Nguyên Giáp](/wiki/V%C3%B5_Nguy%C3%AAn_Gi%C3%A1p "Võ Nguyên Giáp")". However, this may lead to pronunciation distortions that even Vietnamese speakers cannot accurately verify when the name is transliterated back into Vietnamese with diacritics. For example, surname [*Đoàn*](/wiki/Duan_(surname) "Duan (surname)") ([[ɗʷà:n]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Vietnamese "Help:IPA/Vietnamese")) vs [*Doãn*](/wiki/Y%C7%90n_(surname) "Yǐn (surname)") ([[zʷǎ:ˀn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Vietnamese "Help:IPA/Vietnamese")), both become *Doan* when diacritics are omitted and can only distinguish by [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") or [respelling](/wiki/Pronunciation_respelling_for_English "Pronunciation respelling for English").

Based on the Vietnamese custom of addressing individuals by the last monosyllable of their primary name, the English-language *[Chicago Manual of Style](/wiki/Chicago_Manual_of_Style "Chicago Manual of Style")* indexes Vietnamese names according to the "primary-name, surname middle-name", with a cross-reference placed in regards to the surname. [*Ngô Đình Diệm*](/wiki/Ng%C3%B4_%C4%90%C3%ACnh_Di%E1%BB%87m "Ngô Đình Diệm") would be listed as "Diem, Ngo Dinh" and *Võ Nguyên Giáp* would be listed as "Giap, Vo Nguyen".[[9]](#cite_note-9) In Vietnamese-language sources, names are also generally organized in this manner.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

However, indexing the name in the form "Giap, Vo Nguyen" can be misleading as it suggests that "Võ Nguyên" is the surname. Likewise, when the comma is omitted such as "Giap Vo Nguyen", it may also confuse Western readers by implying that the middle name "Nguyên" is the "last name" because it appears at the end of the name, whereas in reality the person’s actual surname is solely "[Võ](/wiki/V%C3%B5 "Võ")". Nowadays, to avoid problems caused by mistakes with surnames, Vietnamese names in English are commonly indexed according to "middle-name primary-name surname" in the [Western order](/wiki/Western_name_order "Western name order") such as "Nguyen Giap Vo" instead of "Giap Vo Nguyen", to determine exactly the surname "Vo", which corresponds to the true "last name" in English texts (especially in media such as TV on-screen graphics, websites, and social media at sports events). At the very least, this helps Western readers identify the correct surname "Võ", rather than mistaking the middle name "Nguyen" for the surname and confusing it with "[Nguyễn](/wiki/Nguyen "Nguyen")".

* In recent years, many English-language sports programs and events have [visually emphasized](/wiki/Emphasis_(typography) "Emphasis (typography)") surnames by through bolding or [all capitalization](/wiki/All_caps "All caps") such as "Nguyen Giap VO". However, even when emphasis is applied, but mistakenly highlighting with the middle name as well can still cause misunderstanding among readers, due to the custom of addressing people by the final syllable of the primary name in Vietnamese culture. For example, emphasizing incorrectly such as "Giap VO NGUYEN" or "VO NGUYEN Giap" may be misinterpreted as a [compound surnames](/wiki/Compound_surnames "Compound surnames"), whereas the correct emphasis should be "Nguyen Giap VO" or "VO Nguyen Giap", because surname is solely "Võ". Similarly, in instances of [alphabetization by surname with comma](/wiki/Comma#In_names "Comma") ([lexical name order](/wiki/Lexical_name_order "Lexical name order")) according to the "surname, given name", his name must be indexed correctly as "Vo, Nguyen Giap", and not as "Vo Nguyen, Giap".
  + In the Olympics, name of swimmer *[Nguyễn Thị Ánh Viên](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Th%E1%BB%8B_%C3%81nh_Vi%C3%AAn "Nguyễn Thị Ánh Viên")* was incorrectly displayed on TV on-screen graphics as "Vien NGUYEN THI ANH" at the Rio 2016[[11]](#cite_note-11) and "Anh Vien NGUYEN THI ANH" at the Tokyo 2020.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Her name must be displayed correctly as "Thi Anh Vien NGUYEN" or "NGUYEN Thi Anh Vien", because her surname is solely "Nguyễn".
* The traditional [Eastern order](/wiki/Eastern_name_order "Eastern name order"), such as "Vo Nguyen Giap"*,* is also applied, mainly when using English in Vietnam. However in [Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games")' events, while Chinese, Korean, Japanese athletes' name is displayed in the format of "SURNAME Given-name", Vietnamese athletes' name is still displayed in "Given-name SURNAME" on TV on-screen graphics, due to Vietnam has not yet been included in the countries list of *Use and rules of each participant name > TV Name "Switching"* in the document *Language Guidelines & Participant Names* of the Olympic Data Feed,[[13]](#cite_note-13) which has included almost countries in the [East Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/East_Asian_Cultural_Sphere "East Asian Cultural Sphere") such as China, Korea, and Japan (formerly Japan was also treated with the Western order until has been included since 2020). Conversely, in almost badminton tournaments of [BWF](/wiki/Badminton_World_Federation "Badminton World Federation")'s system, some players' name from Vietnam is displayed in the traditional Eastern order, while Japan's one is not.
  + For example, name of badminton player [*Nguyễn Thuỳ Linh*](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Th%C3%B9y_Linh "Nguyễn Thùy Linh") is displayed as "Thuy Linh NGUYEN" in Olympics and "NGUYEN Thuy Linh" in BWF's tournaments.

Due to the high frequency of the same surnames in Vietnamese names (having around one-third of the Vietnamese people using the surname [Nguyễn](/wiki/Nguyen "Nguyen")), it has also become more popular to be referred by given name in English. For example, *Nguyễn Tiến Minh* can be referred to as "(Mr.) Tiến Minh" or simply as "(Mr.) Minh". Addressing by surname such as "(Mr.) Nguyễn" is also used, but less commonly when using English in Vietnam.

Additionally, there are certain cases in which Vietnamese personal names when written in full-name form in English, are rearranged symmetrically around the middle name by swapping the surname and the final syllable of the given name, following the English-style order of "first-middle-last". This stems from a confusion that Vietnamese middle names, due to be located at the "middle", would be equivalent to Western middle names. For example, *Võ Nguyên Giáp* is reordered as "Giap Nguyen Vo", *Trần Lê Quốc Toàn* is reordered as "Toan Le Quoc Tran". However, as [mentioned earlier](#Middle_name_(padding_name)), the issue here is not about its position, but the fundamentally different ways in which Vietnamese middle names and Western middle names are used:

* Vietnamese middle names are not used independently but serve only as the prefix to complement the primary name; therefore, it should precede the primary name such as "Nguyen Giap" and "Le Quoc Toan". If not, this arrangement breaks the standard structure of Vietnamese given name such as "Giap Nguyen" and "Toan Le Quoc". This differs from tradional English given names, in which middle names come after the first name.
* Moreover, this ordering can lead to misunderstandings about given name when dealing with different types of official documents. For instance, some forms provide a dedicated "Middle Name" field (which is usually optional to fill), while others (such as the IELTS registration form) do not, this may result in the given name being recorded as "Giap Nguyen" or "Giap" (omitted middle name) in a document, while being recorded as "Nguyen Giap" in another.

Consequently, when a Vietnamese personal name is written in full-name form following Western order, it is often recommended that it be reordered in "middle-name primary-name surname", meaning just focus on that only the surname (in bold) needs to be moved to the last position, while all parts of the given name (underlined) should be left as is, such as ***Võ** Nguyên Giáp* is reordered as "Nguyen Giap **Vo**", ***Trần** Lê Quốc Toàn* is reordered as "Le Quoc Toan **Tran**". Along with this, when filling in the form of 3-name fields "First Name / Middle Name / Last Name", it is advisable to leave the "Middle Name" field blank and enter the entire "middle-name primary-name" sequence in the "First Name" field, such as "Le Quoc Toan / (blank) / Tran" instead of "Toan / Le Quoc /Tran". This practice helps preserve the Vietnamese given name in its proper form, minimizes complications arising from inconsistent ordering of the given name’s monosyllabic elements across different documents, and remains consistent with cases of surname-based alphabetization in lexical name order which uses the "surname, given name" format, such as "Tran, Le Quoc Toan" rather than "Tran, Toan Le Quoc" (see in *[Presentation in passport and paperworks](#Presentation_in_passport_and_paperworks)*).

### Abbreviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Abbreviation")]

There are three common types to abbreviate a Vietnamese name:

1. Informal: keep the last syllable in full, and abbreviate remains in [initials](/wiki/Given_name#Initials "Given name").
2. Formal: keep the last two syllables in full, and abbreviate remains in initials.[[A]](#cite_note-abbr-14)[[B]](#cite_note-Thị-15)
3. Keep surname in full, and abbreviate remains in initials. Often used in English if surname in full is mandatory.

2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Thị_15-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Thị_15-1) The middle name *Thị* (氏) which historically means "a person of this family line", has fallen out of favor among modern Vietnamese women. Many who have *Thị* as a middle name now prefer to abbreviate it in writing. For example, *Nguyễn T. Liễu* instead of *N. Thị Liễu*, or omit it entirely, referring to themselves simply as *Nguyễn Liễu*.

### Presentation in passport and paperworks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vietnamese_name&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Presentation in passport and paperworks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/S.R._Viet_Nam_passport_-_early_2023_revision.png/250px-S.R._Viet_Nam_passport_-_early_2023_revision.png)](/wiki/File:S.R._Viet_Nam_passport_-_early_2023_revision.png)

The contemporary layout of the [Vietnamese passport](/wiki/Vietnamese_passport "Vietnamese passport"), with the standard designation of the person's name.

Since 2023, names in [Vietnamese passports](/wiki/Vietnamese_passport "Vietnamese passport") have been split into two lines: "surname" and "given names", with the middle name treated as a part with the primary name in order to match the format of "given names".[[14]](#cite_note-16)

However, because almost all Vietnamese surnames are [monosyllabic](/wiki/Monosyllable "Monosyllable") and little attention is given to [compound surnames](/wiki/Compound_surnames "Compound surnames") (such as [*Hoàng Phủ*](/wiki/Ho%C3%A0ng_Ph%E1%BB%A7 "Hoàng Phủ"), [*Tôn Thất*](/wiki/T%C3%B4n_Th%E1%BA%A5t "Tôn Thất"), [*Tôn Nữ*](/wiki/Ton_nu "Ton nu"), [*Âu Dương*](/wiki/Ouyang "Ouyang")), the first syllable of a compound surname is often designated as the sole surname, while the second syllable is treated as a middle name. For example:

* "Hoàng | Phủ Ngọc Tường" instead of "Hoàng Phủ | Ngọc Tường", due to confusion between *Hoàng Phủ* and *[Hoàng](/wiki/Ho%C3%A0ng "Hoàng")*.
* "Tôn | Nữ Thị Ninh" instead of "Tôn Nữ | Thị Ninh" , due to confusion between *Tôn Nữ* and *[Tôn](/wiki/T%C3%B4n "Tôn")*.

Vietnamese people do not have the practice of using [hyphens](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen") (e.g., Hoàng-Phủ Ngọc-Tường) or any other markers to indicate the "partition" of their names, and they may sometimes omit their middle names in Western contexts for simplified translation or presentation. The more syllables a name has, the more complicated it becomes to fill it into the required fields.

The following shows ways to fill Vietnamese names in Western paperwork. The entries highlighted in the green background indicate the recommended method which is to leave the "Middle Name" field blank and fill all parts of given name including "middle-name primary-name" into "First Name" field, to avoid issues such as surname confusion or incorrect order of Vietnamese given name (ensure that the middle name is always positioned before the primary name as same as in Vietnamese) in procedures like visa applications or airline ticketing.

Western presentations of the 3-syllable name *"Nguyễn Tiến Minh"*

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Tien Minh | *(blank)* | Nguyen | Tien Minh Nguyen (Nguyen, Tien Minh) | |
| Minh | Tien | Nguyen | Minh Tien Nguyen (Nguyen, Minh Tien) | Minh Nguyen (Nguyen, Minh) |
| Without "middle name" field | Given Name | | Surname | Full presentation | Presentation excluded middle name |
| Tien Minh | | Nguyen | Tien Minh Nguyen (Nguyen, Tien Minh) | |

Western presentations of the 4-syllable name *"Nguyễn Thị Ánh Viên"*

| With "middle name" field | First Name | Middle Name (optional) | Last Name | Full presentation | Presentation excluded middle name |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Thi Anh Vien | *(blank)* | Nguyen | Thi Anh Vien Nguyen (Nguyen, Thi Anh Vien) | |
| Anh Vien | Thi | Nguyen | Anh Vien Thi Nguyen (Nguyen, Anh Vien Thi) | Anh Vien Nguyen (Nguyen, Anh Vien) |
| Vien | Thi Anh | Nguyen | Vien Thi Anh Nguyen (Nguyen, Vien Thi Anh) | Vien Nguyen (Nguyen, Vien) |
| Without "middle name" field | Given Name | | Surname | Full presentation | Presentation excluded middle name |
